content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 3 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in 1 Corinthians Chapter 3 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 3:1 -- "people of the flesh / fleshly ones / belonged to this world" and "infants in Christ"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I...was not able to speak to you as to spiritual [ones] but as to fleshly [ones], as to infants in Christos" | sarkinois as "fleshly [ones]"; preserves dative structure |
| LOGOS | "I...could not address you as spiritual people, but as people of the flesh, as infants in Christ" | sarkinois as "people of the flesh" |
| PNEUMA | "I couldn't talk to you as I would to spiritual people. I had to talk as though you belonged to this world or as though you were infants in Christ" | "belonged to this world" for sarkinois; adds "I had to talk as though" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "People of the flesh" preserves the sarx-reference that is theologically loaded in Paul. PNEUMA's "belonged to this world" is too vague -- sarkinois specifically denotes fleshly composition/orientation, not mere worldliness. EMET notes the important textual variant: sarkinois (v.1, made of flesh) vs. sarkikoi (v.3, characterized by flesh).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's observation on the sarkinois/sarkikoi distinction noted.

---

## 3:2 -- "milk/solid food" structural differences

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Milk I gave you to drink, not solid food; for you were not yet able. But neither now yet are you able" | Preserves Greek word order (milk first); emphatic "neither now yet" |
| LOGOS | "I fed you with milk, not solid food, for you were not ready for it. And even now you are not yet ready" | English word order; "ready" for dynaomai |
| PNEUMA | "I had to feed you with milk, not with solid food, because you weren't ready for anything stronger. And you still aren't ready" | Adds "had to"; "anything stronger" expands; "still aren't ready" condenses |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The metaphor is clear without PNEUMA's expansions ("had to," "anything stronger"). EMET's preservation of Greek word order ("Milk I gave you") is effective for emphasis but not natural in English prose.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:3 -- "of the flesh / fleshly / controlled by your sinful nature" and "behaving only in a human way / walking according to man / living like people of the world"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "for you are still fleshly. For where [there is] among you jealousy and strife, are you not fleshly and walking according to man?" | sarkikoi as "fleshly"; kata anthropon peripateo as "walking according to man" |
| LOGOS | "for you are still of the flesh...are you not of the flesh and behaving only in a human way?" | sarkikoi as "of the flesh"; kata anthropon as "in a human way" |
| PNEUMA | "for you are still controlled by your sinful nature. You are jealous of one another and you quarrel with each other. Doesn't that prove you are controlled by your sinful nature? Aren't you living like people of the world?" | sarkikoi as "controlled by your sinful nature"; breaks into multiple sentences; adds "doesn't that prove" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Of the flesh" preserves the connection to Paul's flesh/spirit framework without the theological loading of PNEUMA's "sinful nature" (which imports a specific anthropological interpretation). PNEUMA's breaking into multiple rhetorical questions adds material. LOGOS's "behaving only in a human way" captures kata anthropon effectively.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "sinful nature" for sarx flagged as an over-interpretation.

---

## 3:4 -- "merely human / men / people of the world"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "are you not men?" | anthropoi as "men" (literal) |
| LOGOS | "are you not being merely human?" | anthropoi rendered dynamically with "merely" |
| PNEUMA | "aren't you acting just like people of the world?" | "people of the world" expands far beyond anthropoi |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Merely human" captures Paul's point: the Corinthians are acting at a purely human level, without the Spirit's influence. EMET's "men" is literal but loses the pejorative connotation. PNEUMA's "people of the world" adds a concept not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:5 -- "Servants / God's servants" and "as the Lord assigned/gave"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Servants through whom you believed, and to each as the Lord gave" | diakonoi as "servants"; didomi as "gave" |
| LOGOS | "Servants through whom you believed, as the Lord assigned to each" | didomi as "assigned" |
| PNEUMA | "We are only God's servants through whom you believed the Good News. Each of us did the work the Lord gave us." | Adds "only" and "God's"; adds "the Good News"; restructures second clause |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Assigned" interprets didomi in its delegative sense. PNEUMA adds "only," "God's," and "the Good News," none present in the Greek. The simplicity of the original is important -- Paul deflates apostolic status, and additions like "God's servants" partially re-inflate it.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:6 -- "gave the growth / made it grow"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I planted, Apollos watered, but God was causing [it] to grow" | Imperfect tense "was causing to grow" |
| LOGOS | "I planted, Apollos watered, but God gave the growth" | Aorist interpretation "gave the growth" |
| PNEUMA | "I planted the seed in your hearts, and Apollos watered it, but it was God who made it grow" | Adds "the seed in your hearts"; "made it grow" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Gave the growth" correctly renders the imperfect auxano, which Paul uses to contrast with the aorist verbs for planting and watering -- God's growth-giving is ongoing. PNEUMA's "the seed in your hearts" is an interpretive addition that makes the metaphor more explicit than Paul's compressed original.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:9 -- "fellow workers / co-workers / God's workers"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "we are God's fellow workers; you are God's field, God's building" | theou synergoi as "God's fellow workers" |
| LOGOS | "we are God's fellow workers. You are God's field, God's building" | Same rendering |
| PNEUMA | "we are both God's workers. And you are God's field. You are God's building" | Drops "fellow" from synergoi; adds "both"; breaks into separate sentences |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Fellow workers" preserves the syn- prefix of synergoi, which indicates collaborative work with God (or with each other for God). PNEUMA's dropping of "fellow" loses this important nuance of partnership.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:10 -- "skilled master builder / wise master builder / expert builder"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "as a wise master builder I laid a foundation" | sophos architekton as "wise master builder" |
| LOGOS | "like a skilled master builder I laid a foundation" | sophos as "skilled" |
| PNEUMA | "I have laid the foundation like an expert builder" | Drops "master"; sophos as "expert"; reorders |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Skilled master builder" captures both sophos (competent, wise) and architekton (chief builder). PNEUMA's "expert builder" loses the architekton distinction between the master builder who designs and the laborers who build upon the design. EMET's "wise" is more literal for sophos but "skilled" works better in the construction metaphor context.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:13 -- "the Day will disclose it / fire will reveal" and "what sort of work"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the work of each will become manifest; for the day will reveal [it], because in fire it is revealed, and the fire itself will test the work of each, what sort it is" | Close to Greek clause structure |
| LOGOS | "each one's work will become manifest, for the Day will disclose it, because it will be revealed by fire, and the fire will test what sort of work each one has done" | Capitalizes "Day" for eschatological reference |
| PNEUMA | "on the judgment day, fire will reveal what kind of work each builder has done. The fire will show if a person's work has any value" | Adds "judgment"; "builder" for "each one"; "show if...has any value" interprets the testing |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Capitalizing "Day" effectively signals the eschatological Day of the Lord without PNEUMA's explicit "judgment day" (the Greek says simply he hemera, "the Day"). PNEUMA's "show if a person's work has any value" over-interprets -- the fire tests "what sort" (hopoion) the work is, not merely whether it has value.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:15 -- "suffer loss / suffer great loss" and "as through fire / barely escaping through a wall of flames"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he will suffer loss, but he himself will be saved, yet so as through fire" | Literal rendering of zemiothesetai and hos dia pyros |
| LOGOS | "he will suffer loss, though he himself will be saved, but only as through fire" | Adds "only" for emphasis |
| PNEUMA | "the builder will suffer great loss. The builder will be saved, but like someone barely escaping through a wall of flames" | Adds "great"; "barely escaping through a wall of flames" expands the simile dramatically |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "But only as through fire" adds legitimate emphasis to hos ("as, like"). PNEUMA's expansion to "barely escaping through a wall of flames" is vivid but far exceeds the Greek, which is a terse simile. "Great loss" adds an adjective not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:16 -- "Do you not know / Don't you realize"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Do you not know that you are [the] temple of God and the Spirit of God dwells in you?" | Standard literal rendering |
| LOGOS | "Do you not know that you are God's temple and that God's Spirit dwells in you?" | "God's temple" (possessive) for clarity |
| PNEUMA | "Don't you realize that all of you together are the temple of God and that the Spirit of God lives in you?" | Adds "all of you together" to emphasize plural; "realize" for "know"; "lives" for "dwells" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's addition of "all of you together" is interpretively correct (the "you" is plural in Greek) but adds explicit material. "Dwells" (oikeo) is more precise than "lives" for the theological concept of divine indwelling. The consensus text carries a note about the plural "you."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:19 -- "craftiness / cleverness / own cleverness"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The [one] catching the wise in the craftiness of them" | panourgia as "craftiness" |
| LOGOS | "He catches the wise in their craftiness" | Same term, smoother English |
| PNEUMA | "He traps the wise in the snare of their own cleverness" | Adds "snare of"; "own"; panourgia as "cleverness" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Craftiness" is a better rendering of panourgia (which has negative connotations of cunning) than "cleverness" (PNEUMA, which can be positive). PNEUMA's addition of "snare of" and "own" expands beyond the Job 5:13 citation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:20 -- "thoughts / reasonings" for dialogismous

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The Lord knows the reasonings of the wise, that they are futile" | dialogismous as "reasonings" |
| LOGOS | "The Lord knows the thoughts of the wise, that they are futile" | dialogismous as "thoughts" |
| PNEUMA | "The Lord knows the thoughts of the wise; he knows they are worthless" | dialogismous as "thoughts"; adds "he knows"; "worthless" for mataioi |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Thoughts" for dialogismous is standard, though EMET's "reasonings" captures the deliberative sense of the word (inner arguments, calculations). "Futile" for mataioi is more precise than "worthless" (PNEUMA), as mataioi means vain/purposeless rather than without value.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's "reasonings" noted as a valuable alternative.

---

## 3:22-23 -- "Cephas/Kephas/Peter" and "things about to come / the future"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Paulos or Apollos or Kephas or [the] world or life or death or [things] present or [things] about to come -- all are yours, and you [are] of Christos, and Christos [is] of God" | Transliterated names; "things about to come" for mellonta |
| LOGOS | "whether Paul or Apollos or Cephas or the world or life or death or the present or the future -- all are yours, and you are Christ's, and Christ is God's" | Anglicized; "the future" for mellonta |
| PNEUMA | "whether Paul or Apollos or Peter, or the world, or life and death, or the present and the future. Everything belongs to you, and you belong to Christ, and Christ belongs to God" | "Peter" for Kephas; "belongs to" makes implicit explicit; "Everything" repeats for emphasis |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Cephas" is Paul's consistent name for Peter throughout his letters and should be preserved. PNEUMA's "Peter" harmonizes with the Gospels but loses Paul's distinctive Aramaic usage. LOGOS's "the future" effectively captures mellonta. PNEUMA's "belongs to" is interpretively helpful but adds explicitness beyond the terse Greek genitives.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. "Cephas" retained over "Peter."

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA adds interpretive material | vv.1, 2, 5, 6, 13, 15, 16, 19, 22-23 | "controlled by sinful nature," "the seed in your hearts," "barely escaping through a wall of flames" |
| PNEUMA over-interprets technical Pauline vocabulary | vv.1, 3 | sarkinois/sarkikoi as "sinful nature"; sarx theology obscured |
| LOGOS-SOPHIA near-identity | Most verses | LOGOS as base text for consensus |
| EMET preserves textual variants and lexical precision | vv.1, 20, 22 | sarkinois vs. sarkikoi distinction; "reasonings" for dialogismous |
| Name form divergence | vv.4, 22 | Kephas/Cephas/Peter |
| Metaphor expansion by PNEUMA | vv.10, 15, 19 | "expert builder," "wall of flames," "snare of cleverness" |

**Overall Pattern Analysis:** Chapter 3 reveals the same translator dynamics as chapters 1-2. LOGOS continues to serve as the effective base for SOPHIA's consensus. PNEUMA's most significant divergence in this chapter is the rendering of sarx vocabulary ("sinful nature" for sarkikoi in v.3), which imports a specific theological interpretation that narrows Paul's broader flesh/Spirit framework. EMET's most valuable contribution is the sarkinois/sarkikoi textual observation in v.1. PNEUMA's vivid expansions of metaphors (particularly v.15, "barely escaping through a wall of flames") are homiletically effective but exceed the boundaries of translation.

---
